design.gap: G — Scheduling / workflows / state machines: no cron, no delayed actions, no transition semantics; events are synchronous-on-write only (defer()-based fan-out in lib/events.ts emitEntryEvent).
design.approach:
The foundation is one boring pg table: `jobs` (projectId, kind, payload jsonb, dedupeKey, runAt, status, attempts/maxAttempts, claimedAt, lastError) in db/schema.ts, pushed via the existing `npm run db:push` workflow. The runner claims work with a SINGLE SQL statement — `UPDATE jobs SET status='running', claimed_at=now(), attempts=attempts+1 WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE status='pending' AND run_at<=now() ORDER BY run_at LIMIT $n FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) RETURNING *` — because the neon-http driver (db/index.ts) has no interactive transactions; one statement = one implicit transaction, exactly the pattern updateEntryIf already proves out. Retries are job-level (exponential run_at reschedule to maxAttempts) plus a stale-lease reclaim. Draining is host-agnostic plain HTTP: `POST /api/jobs/drain` gated by CRON_SECRET with hardened auth — the route returns 503 E_UNCONFIGURED before reading any header when CRON_SECRET is missing or under 16 chars (so an unset env can never degrade to accepting 'Bearer undefined'), and compares via crypto.timingSafeEqual over equal-length buffers. Netlify scheduled function today, Render cron curl tomorrow; an opportunistic `defer(() => drainDueJobs())` nudge at the MCP and delivery mutation routes keeps latency low. Handlers live in lib/job-handlers.ts keyed by kind.

Delayed actions extend the existing declarative EventAction with `after: "3d"|"12h"|"45m"`. At emit time, `after` actions are enqueued as 'event_action' jobs whose payload carries only REFERENCES plus a stable actionHash (sha256 of the canonical-JSON action) — the queued payload is never the execution authority. At run time the handler re-resolves everything against current truth: it re-fetches the entry (gone → skip-as-succeeded), re-fetches the collection and locates the action in collection.events[event] by actionHash (absent, edited, or disabled:true → skip-as-succeeded), then re-evaluates `when` against the fresh entry via matchesClauses. Config therefore remains the single source of truth even for work queued 365 days out: disabling, removing, or editing an action is a true declarative kill switch for pending delayed sends, stated verbatim in the define_collection events description. Execution funnels into the same deliverWebhook/sendEmailAction, so every outcome lands in webhook_deliveries and refire_delivery works unchanged. Recurring schedules get `project_schedules` (preset recurrence objects, no cron strings) ticked by the drain endpoint with dedupeKey'd 'schedule_fire' jobs and CAS-advanced nextRunAt.

State machines are collection-level JSONB config: `workflow: {field: <enum field>, initial, transitions: [{from: string|string[], to, actors?: ('mcp'|'admin'|'delivery')[], actions?: EventAction[]}]}`. Enforcement is closed over EVERY write path: the create rule (default the field to `initial`; explicit non-initial → E_VALIDATION) lives in one shared applyWorkflowOnCreate helper invoked by BOTH createEntry and bulkCreateEntries' per-item loop — the two db.insert(entries) sites — so bulk_create_entries cannot spoof state. Transitions are actor-gated using the AuditActor already passed to every mutator: `actors` defaults to ['mcp','admin'], meaning delivery (end-user) writes cannot drive a transition unless the tenant opts that transition in — the flagship approval flow is secure by default, and the tool description states the boundary out loud. Define-time validation rejects ambiguity (no two transitions may share an overlapping (from, to) pair), so a successful A→B change always resolves to exactly one transition. Both update paths reject a target value that is no transition's `to` with E_VALIDATION (listing valid targets) BEFORE any SQL is built — no empty IN() lists. Atomicity: updateEntry appends `data->>field = A` to its WHERE; updateEntryIf pushes `data->>field IN (actor-filtered allowedFroms)` into its conditions and recovers the exact pre-image in the same single statement via a self-join UPDATE (`UPDATE entries SET ... FROM entries old WHERE entries.id = old.id AND entries.id = $1 ... RETURNING old.data`), so the transitioned payload's `from` is exact even on the CAS path. Matched-transition actions fire through runEventAction with payload {event:'entry.transitioned', transition:{field,from,to}, entry, previous?}.

keyDecisions.decision: Single-statement FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED claim in a plain pg `jobs` table, drained by an HTTP endpoint hardened with fail-closed CRON_SECRET auth (503 when unset/short, timingSafeEqual compare)
keyDecisions.rationale: neon-http supports one implicit transaction per statement — the constraint updateEntryIf already designs around; SKIP LOCKED makes concurrent drains safe with zero coordination; HTTP drain is callable identically from any host's cron. Fail-closed auth is required because /api/jobs is excluded from Clerk middleware, making the secret compare the ONLY gate on an endpoint that executes cross-project side effects — a naive `=== \`Bearer ${env}\`` compare degrades to the guessable literal 'Bearer undefined' when the env var is missing.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: pg-boss/graphile-worker (persistent connections + LISTEN/NOTIFY, incompatible with the HTTP driver), vendor-native scheduled-function logic (host coupling), and simple string comparison of the bearer (timing side channel + unset-env bypass).

keyDecisions.decision: Queued 'event_action' job payloads carry references + a stable actionHash, never an executable action copy: the handler re-resolves the action from the CURRENT collection.events at run time and skips-as-succeeded when it is absent, edited, or disabled
keyDecisions.rationale: Rule 1 says config is the source of truth; an enqueue-time copy silently outranking it means disabled:true stops being 'paused' for anything already queued — up to a year of un-cancellable sends at after:'365d'. Run-time re-resolution makes disable/remove/edit true declarative kill switches, symmetric with the run-time `when` re-evaluation, at the cost of one collection fetch the handler was doing anyway. Hash matching also gives edit semantics for free: an edited action orphans (skips) jobs queued under the old definition, which is the least-surprising behavior and is stated in the events description.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Executing from the payload copy with a 'disabled re-check' (vacuous — the copy was captured when disabled was false, so the check can never skip), and the reviewer-suggested hybrid of falling back to the payload copy on hash miss (reintroduces exactly the staleness being fixed; a hash miss must mean skip, deterministically).

keyDecisions.decision: Workflow create-rule lives in one shared applyWorkflowOnCreate(collection, clean) helper called from BOTH createEntry and bulkCreateEntries' per-item validation loop
keyDecisions.rationale: bulkCreateEntries is a parallel write path (its own validate() + multi-row insert at lib/entries.ts:415; it never calls createEntry), so placing enforcement only in createEntry leaves an MCP-reachable spoof hole that directly violates Rule 5 and the tool description's own claim. One helper, two call sites, per-item E_VALIDATION in bulk results — the same 'one definition of valid' discipline buildEntrySchema already establishes.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Folding the rule into buildEntrySchema itself — tempting, but the schema compiler is collection-config-agnostic today (fields[] in, zod out) and threading workflow through it couples validation.ts to a collections-level concern; a helper at the validate() call sites keeps layering clean while still covering every insert.

keyDecisions.decision: Per-transition `actors` gate defaulting to ['mcp','admin'] — delivery (end-user) writes cannot execute a transition unless that transition explicitly opts in with actors including 'delivery'
keyDecisions.rationale: Delivery PATCH and admin saveEntry both funnel into updateEntry, so without actor gating an entry OWNER can self-approve and fire the approval email — the flagship use case would be insecure as designed. The AuditActor is already threaded through every mutator, so enforcement is a filter on which transitions' froms are admissible, before SQL is built. Defaulting delivery to excluded makes the safe thing the default; the define_collection workflow description states the default and the opt-in out loud (Rule 1), including the caveat that 'admin' covers client-role members in v1.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: The reviewer-suggested default of 'all actors' with the boundary merely documented — rejected because it ships the self-approval hole as default behavior; declarative platforms should be secure by default and loosened explicitly. Also rejected: a separate deliveryAllowed:true boolean (subsumed by the actors array at no extra grammar cost).

keyDecisions.decision: CAS-path transitions recover the exact pre-image via a single-statement self-join UPDATE (RETURNING old.data), and define-time validation rejects transitions with overlapping (from, to) pairs
keyDecisions.rationale: Without a pre-read, `from` is unknowable on updateEntryIf (the documented lib/entries.ts:316 gotcha) and multiple transitions sharing a `to` would make the matched transition — hence which actions fire — undecidable. Postgres's UPDATE ... FROM reads the joined row from the pre-update snapshot, so `RETURNING old.data` yields the pre-image in one implicit transaction, satisfying the neon-http constraint. With ambiguity rejected at define time, (from=A, to=B) resolves exactly one transition; both update paths reject a B that is no transition's target with E_VALIDATION before any SQL exists (no empty IN() lists).
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Restricting v1 to fire actions only when `from` is unambiguous (leaves `from` unreported on the CAS path and creates a confusing sometimes-fires surface), and a pre-read before the CAS UPDATE (two statements = a race window, the exact thing updateEntryIf exists to avoid).

keyDecisions.decision: Reuse EventAction as the universal action vocabulary — `after` is a knob on EventActionBase; delayed/scheduled/transition actions all execute through deliverWebhook/sendEmailAction
keyDecisions.rationale: One action grammar means one validation path, one outcome log (webhook_deliveries), one replay tool (refire_delivery), and tool descriptions that compose instead of multiply; 'every delivery attempt ends in a log row' is inherited for free.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: A separate 'scheduled task' action type with its own dispatch/log/replay machinery — parallel implementations that would drift, like the matchesClauses/buildWhere gotcha but worse.

keyDecisions.decision: New `jobs` table rather than evolving webhook_deliveries into a queue
keyDecisions.rationale: webhook_deliveries is an immutable outcome log (refire appends) with a TEXT attempts column; a mutable claimed/rescheduled queue row breaks that contract and every log consumer. Jobs (mutable intent) and deliveries (immutable outcome) are different lifecycles; a job run still writes its delivery row.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Adding nextAttemptAt/status='pending' to webhook_deliveries — fewer tables but corrupts the log's semantics and needs an attempts-type migration under live readers.

keyDecisions.decision: Recurrence presets ({frequency, at, weekday, dayOfMonth, timezone}) instead of cron expressions; opportunistic drain nudges live in API routes via defer(), not inside lib/events.ts
keyDecisions.rationale: Presets match the platform's access-rules philosophy, are self-describing via enums, and validate with zod — no cron-parser dep. Nudging from routes avoids the import cycle (drain imports job-handlers which imports events) and defer() already guarantees post-response execution.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Raw cron strings (opaque in JSON Schema, non-repairable errors) and tail-calling drain from emitEntryEvent (cyclic imports, couples emitter to executor).

increments.name: pg jobs primitive: table, claim/retry runner, hardened drain endpoint, list_jobs
increments.what: Add `jobs` table to db/schema.ts (id, projectId FK cascade, kind text, payload jsonb, dedupeKey text with partial unique index (kind, dedupeKey) WHERE dedupe_key IS NOT NULL AND status='pending', runAt timestamptz default now, status pending|running|succeeded|failed|canceled, attempts int default 0, maxAttempts int default 5, claimedAt, lastError, createdAt/updatedAt; index (status, runAt)). New lib/jobs.ts: enqueueJob() with .onConflictDoNothing() on the dedupe index; claimDueJobs(n) as ONE raw-templated UPDATE...WHERE id IN (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED)...RETURNING (parameterized values, fixed identifiers — updateEntryIf discipline); reclaimStale() (running + claimed_at < now()-120s → pending if attempts<maxAttempts else failed); finishJob(id, ok|error) with run_at=now+min(2^attempts*30s,1h); drainJobs(handlers,{maxJobs=10,budgetMs=15000}) with per-job try/catch; listJobs(projectId, filter) limit+1 idiom. New lib/job-handlers.ts: HANDLERS record with built-in 'noop' kind. New app/api/jobs/drain/route.ts with FAIL-CLOSED auth: (1) if !process.env.CRON_SECRET || CRON_SECRET.length < 16 → 503 {error:'drain not configured — set CRON_SECRET (min 16 chars)', code:'E_UNCONFIGURED'} BEFORE reading the Authorization header (append E_UNCONFIGURED to ERROR_CODES — append-only per convention); (2) parse bearer via bearerFrom, compare against the secret with crypto.timingSafeEqual over equal-length buffers (length mismatch → immediate 401), never template the env var into an expected-header string; then reclaimStale + tick hook + drainJobs, return {claimed, succeeded, failed, rescheduled}. Add /api/jobs to middleware.ts matcher exclusions. Opportunistic nudge: defer(() => drainDueJobs()) after tools/call in app/api/mcp/route.ts and after POST create in app/api/v1/[collection]/route.ts (cheap SELECT EXISTS due guard first). New MCP tool list_jobs (TOOL_DEFS + callTool case + zod args {kind?, status?, limit?, offset?}); description states jobs come only from declarative features, never arbitrary code. Netlify glue: netlify/functions/jobs-drain.mjs (scheduled '* * * * *') + netlify.toml stanza — the only host-specific piece. Smoke (scripts/smoke/jobs.test.mjs): noop enqueue via direct SQL → drain → status transitions; dedupeKey suppression; list_jobs over MCP; AUTH cases: drain with wrong secret → 401, drain with header 'Bearer undefined' while a helper-spawned server has CRON_SECRET unset → 503 not execution (plus a unit-level assertion that the route rejects before header read when unconfigured).
increments.surface: New endpoint POST /api/jobs/drain (CRON_SECRET bearer, 503 E_UNCONFIGURED when secret unset/short); new MCP tool list_jobs; new ErrorCode E_UNCONFIGURED; new env var CRON_SECRET
increments.migration: new `jobs` table via `npm run db:push` (repo has no migration files by design)

increments.name: Delayed event actions: `after` on EventAction, run-time re-resolution of action + `when`
increments.what: Extend EventActionBase (db/schema.ts:17-22) with `after?: string` (regex /^\d+(m|h|d)$/, 1m..365d — validated in validateAccessAndEvents with a fix-hint naming accepted units). Extend eventActionSchema (lib/mcp/tools.ts:561-568) identically. New actionHash(action) in lib/events.ts: sha256 of canonical JSON (sorted keys, `after`/`disabled` excluded so pausing doesn't change identity — content fields only... no: include everything EXCEPT `disabled`, so edits to url/to/subject/when/after orphan old jobs while toggling disabled matches). In emitEntryEvent: partition actions — immediate ones unchanged; `after` ones enqueueJob({kind:'event_action', runAt: now+parse(after), dedupeKey: `${entryId}:${event}:${hash}`, payload: {collectionId, collectionName, event, entryId, actionHash: hash, enqueuedAction (display/debug only), changedFields}}). Export runEventAction(collection, event, action, entry, payload) refactored from the dispatch ternary. 'event_action' handler in lib/job-handlers.ts executes ONLY from current truth: (1) load collection by id (direct db select, not the name-keyed cache) — missing → succeed-as-skipped; (2) locate the live action in collection.events[event] by actionHash — absent (removed or edited) or disabled:true → succeed-as-skipped with note 'action removed/disabled since enqueue'; the payload's enqueuedAction copy is NEVER executed, only shown in list_jobs; (3) load entry by id — missing → succeed-as-skipped; (4) evaluate live action.when via matchesClauses against fresh entry.data; (5) dispatch via runEventAction — outcome lands in webhook_deliveries so get_deliveries/refire_delivery work unchanged. Update define_collection events description: 'add after:\'3d\' to defer the action; at send time the action is re-read from the collection config and `when` is re-evaluated against the current entry — disabling, removing, or editing an action also cancels its pending delayed sends; deleted entries skip silently'. Smoke (scripts/smoke/delayed-events.test.mjs): (a) after:'1h' webhook + when[status eq open] → rewind run_at → drain → received; (b) flip status before drain → skipped; (c) define_collection sets disabled:true before drain → skipped; (d) remove the action entirely before drain → skipped.
increments.surface: define_collection events actions accept `after`; description states run-time re-resolution + kill-switch semantics; list_jobs shows pending reminders (enqueued copy display-only); get_deliveries/refire_delivery cover delayed outcomes unchanged

increments.name: Recurring schedules: project_schedules + define/list/delete_schedule tools + tick
increments.what: New `project_schedules` table in db/schema.ts: id, projectId FK cascade, name (unique per project), recurrence jsonb {frequency:'hourly'|'daily'|'weekly'|'monthly', at?:'HH:MM', weekday?, dayOfMonth?:1..28, timezone? IANA default 'UTC'}, action jsonb (EventAction webhook|email; `when`/`after` rejected here with fix-hint), enabled default true, nextRunAt notNull, lastRunAt, createdAt/updatedAt; index (enabled, nextRunAt). New lib/schedules.ts: recurrenceSchema (per-frequency superRefine: weekly needs weekday, monthly needs dayOfMonth ≤28 with hint why 29-31 are rejected), computeNextRun via Intl timezone math, tickSchedules(): select due enabled rows (limit 20) → enqueueJob({kind:'schedule_fire', dedupeKey:`sched:${id}:${nextRunAt.toISOString()}`, payload:{scheduleId, name, action, scheduledFor}}) then CAS-advance UPDATE ... WHERE id=? AND next_run_at=<claimed> (0 rows = lost race = fine, job deduped; advance from now — missed windows fire once, never backfill). Wire tickSchedules() into the drain route before drainJobs. 'schedule_fire' handler: RE-FETCH the schedule row first — deleted or enabled:false → succeed-as-skipped (same run-time-truth rule as G2; the payload action is executed only if the row still exists and is enabled, and the handler compares the row's action to the payload's — mismatch means edited-since-enqueue → skip); webhook → deliverWebhook payload {event:'schedule.fired', schedule:{id,name}, scheduledFor, firedAt} (signed + logged, webhook_deliveries.collectionId made nullable in the same push, logged null); email → dispatchEmail with {{name}}/{{scheduledFor}} interpolation. Three MCP tools: define_schedule (upsert by name; description: minimum frequency hourly, presets only — no cron strings, same webhook/email action vocabulary, at-least-once so receivers must dedupe, fires can be up to a minute late), list_schedules (recurrence, nextRunAt, lastRunAt, enabled), delete_schedule (returns full deleted spec — stated as the reversibility story; enabled:false via define_schedule is the pause path and also skips already-queued fires). Smoke (scripts/smoke/schedules.test.mjs): daily schedule → rewind next_run_at → drain → receiver hit + nextRunAt advanced + dedupe on double-drain; disable-before-drain → queued fire skipped.
increments.surface: New MCP tools define_schedule / list_schedules / delete_schedule; schedule.fired webhooks/emails in get_deliveries; drain endpoint also ticks schedules
increments.migration: new `project_schedules` table + webhook_deliveries.collectionId made nullable, via `npm run db:push`

increments.name: Declarative state machines: workflow config, actor-gated transitions, enforcement closed over ALL write paths
increments.what: New JSONB column collections.workflow (events/access pattern): {field, initial, transitions: [{from: string|string[], to, actors?: ('mcp'|'admin'|'delivery')[], actions?: EventAction[]}]}. New lib/workflow.ts: validateWorkflow (field must be an existing enum field; initial and every from/to in its options — error lists options; REJECT any two transitions whose (from, to) pairs overlap, with a hint naming the colliding pair, so (A,B) always resolves one transition; actions validated exactly like event actions); allowedFroms(workflow, to, actorType) filtering transitions by actors (default ['mcp','admin'] — delivery excluded unless the transition lists 'delivery'); matchTransition(workflow, from, to); applyWorkflowOnCreate(collection, clean) — if the field is absent default it to initial, if present and !== initial throw ValidationError E_VALIDATION `workflow: "<field>" must start at "<initial>" — transitions move it from there`. Enforcement in lib/entries.ts: createEntry calls applyWorkflowOnCreate after validate(); bulkCreateEntries calls it INSIDE the per-item loop (lib/entries.ts:400) so a spoofed item fails per-item with the same message while valid siblings proceed — closing the parallel-write-path hole (only two db.insert(entries) sites exist and both are now covered). updateEntry — when the patch changes the field from current A to B: FIRST, if B is no transition's `to` → E_VALIDATION listing valid target values (before any SQL); if no actor-admissible transition covers A→B → E_VALIDATION `transition "<A>" → "<B>" is not permitted for actor "<type>" — allowed actors: [...]` (delivery callers see it as 422 via the existing ValidationError mapping); else append parameterized `data->>'<field>' = <A>` to the UPDATE's WHERE — 0 rows → E_CONFLICT 'concurrent transition — re-read and retry'. updateEntryIf — if the patch sets the workflow field to B: reject non-target B with the same E_VALIDATION before SQL; compute actor-filtered allowedFroms(B) (empty → the actor-permission E_VALIDATION, never an empty IN()); push `data->>'<field>' IN (allowedFroms)` into conditions AND switch the statement to the self-join form `UPDATE entries SET ... FROM entries old WHERE entries.id = old.id AND entries.id = $1 AND <conditions> RETURNING entries.*, old.data AS previous_data` — still ONE statement (neon-http safe), and old.data is the pre-update snapshot, so the exact `from` (and thus the exact matched transition) is recovered with no pre-read; losing racers get the existing {ok:false, reason:'conflict'}. After a successful A→B on either path, defer firing the matched transition's actions (unique by the define-time ambiguity rule) through runEventAction with payload {event:'entry.transitioned', transition:{field, from:A, to:B}, entry, previous?} — `after`-delayed transition actions ride G2's job path including run-time re-resolution against the current workflow config. MCP surface: defineArgs + TOOL_DEFS define_collection gain `workflow`; the description states the boundaries OUT LOUD: 'initial enforced on every create path including bulk_create_entries and public/delivery writes; illegal transitions rejected with the allowed-targets list; transitions are actor-gated — by default only mcp and admin actors may transition; add "delivery" to a transition's actors to let end users drive it (e.g. submitted→cancelled by the owner); note "admin" includes client-role members in v1'. describe_collection returns workflow; BOUNDARIES constant updated: remove 'no workflows', add 'workflows = declared field transitions + actions, actor-gated; no arbitrary code, no multi-entry orchestration'. Delivery PATCH and admin saveEntry inherit enforcement for free (both call updateEntry with their actor). Smoke (scripts/smoke/workflow.test.mjs): create defaults to initial; spoofed create rejected; spoofed BULK item rejected per-item while sibling succeeds; owner delivery PATCH submitted→approved → 422 (actor gate); a transition with actors:['delivery'] allows the owner PATCH; legal MCP transition fires receiver webhook with exact from/to; illegal transition names allowed targets; B-not-a-target → E_VALIDATION on both update paths.
increments.surface: define_collection/describe_collection gain `workflow` (with actors grammar); BOUNDARIES text updated; entry.transitioned rows in get_deliveries; enforcement visible on MCP create/update/update_entry_if/bulk_create_entries, delivery POST/PATCH, admin form
increments.migration: new collections.workflow JSONB column via `npm run db:push` (JSONB needs no timestamp revival in the unstable_cache wrapper)

increments.name: CAS transition proof: self-join pre-image smoke + race coverage
increments.what: Dedicated verification increment for the two load-bearing single-statement claims, split out so G4 stays M and the risky SQL is proven in isolation before anything composes on it. Extend scripts/smoke/workflow.test.mjs (or a sibling cas-transition.test.mjs): (1) two racing update_entry_if transitions on the same entry — exactly one {ok:true}, the loser gets reason:'conflict', and the fired entry.transitioned delivery reports the exact pre-image `from` (asserting RETURNING old.data reads the pre-update snapshot over neon-http); (2) two transitions sharing `to` with disjoint froms (draft→closed fires emailA config, open→closed fires webhookB) — assert the correct action fires for each starting state, proving matched-transition resolution from the recovered `from`; (3) updateEntryIf with additional user `if` conditions PLUS a workflow guard — both compose in one WHERE; (4) define_collection attempt with overlapping (from,to) pairs → E_VALIDATION naming the collision. If (1) reveals the self-join pre-image is unreliable over the HTTP driver, the specified fallback is a two-step optimistic loop (read A → UPDATE ... WHERE field = A) which keeps exactness at the cost of a retry loop — decision recorded in the test file header either way.
increments.surface: No new surface — hardens/verifies G4's update_entry_if transition semantics end to end

increments.name: Ops polish: cancel_job, admin Automation section, transition-aware entry form
increments.what: New MCP tool cancel_job {id}: single-statement UPDATE jobs SET status='canceled' WHERE id=? AND project_id=? AND status='pending' RETURNING — running/finished jobs return E_CONFLICT 'job already <status> — only pending jobs can be canceled' (lib/jobs.ts cancelJob + TOOL_DEFS + switch case). Description notes cancel_job is the per-job override; the declarative kill switch for delayed event actions is disabling/removing the action itself (G2 semantics). Admin: new 'Automation' settings section (settings/page.tsx + sections.tsx, requireOperator actions) listing pending/failed jobs (kind, runAt, attempts, lastError) with cancel buttons, and schedules with an enable/disable toggle (server action flips project_schedules.enabled) — read paths reuse listJobs/listSchedules, following the delivery-log section idiom. EntryForm (components/EntryForm.tsx enum branch): when the collection has a workflow on this field and the form edits an existing entry, filter select options to current value + targets allowed FROM current FOR THE ADMIN ACTOR (choices computed server-side in the edit page, loadRelationChoices preload idiom); new-entry form pins the field to initial. Validation core remains the enforcer — this is UX truthfulness, not a gate. Smoke: extend jobs.test.mjs with cancel round-trip (enqueue future job, cancel_job over MCP, drain, assert never ran).
increments.surface: New MCP tool cancel_job; admin settings 'Automation' section (jobs + schedule toggles); workflow- and actor-aware enum select in the entry form

design.invariantCompliance: Rule 1 (declarative + self-describing): every capability is config on the collection (events.after, workflow) or a first-class MCP object (schedules), inspected via describe_collection/list_schedules/list_jobs — and config REMAINS the runtime source of truth: delayed jobs and schedule fires re-resolve their action/schedule row at execution time, so disabled/removed/edited config is honored for already-queued work (the events and schedule tool descriptions state this kill-switch semantic explicitly). The workflow description states the actor boundary out loud: delivery actors cannot transition unless a transition opts in via actors:['delivery'], and 'admin' includes client-role members in v1. BOUNDARIES is edited in G4 to stop claiming 'no workflows' and to state the new boundary (declared, actor-gated field transitions + webhook/email actions; no arbitrary code, no multi-entry orchestration); at-least-once delivery, run-time re-evaluation, and preset-only recurrence are all stated in descriptions. Rule 2 (machine-readable errors with fix hints): bad `after` names accepted units/bounds; bad recurrence names the missing preset key; illegal transitions list allowed targets from the current state; actor-blocked transitions name the permitted actors; ambiguous (from,to) definitions name the collision at define time; a non-target B fails E_VALIDATION before SQL on BOTH update paths; transition races return the documented E_CONFLICT; drain misconfiguration returns the new append-only E_UNCONFIGURED with the exact env-var fix in the message; job failures store lastError verbatim, readable via list_jobs. Rule 3 (secrets are references): job payloads carry ids + an actionHash, never secrets and never authoritative action bodies; email jobs resolve the Resend key at run time via connectorSecret; webhook signing fetches webhookSigningSecret inside deliverWebhook at send time; CRON_SECRET lives in env only, compared timing-safely, and the endpoint fails closed (503) when it is absent rather than degrading to a guessable literal. Rule 4 (destructive = plan + confirm): nothing new destroys entry data; dropping the workflow's enum field still routes through the diffFields confirm gate; delete_schedule returns the full spec (stated reversibility); removing workflow config changes future enforcement only. Rule 5 (strict validation never weakens): the workflow create rule lives in one shared applyWorkflowOnCreate invoked by BOTH insert paths (createEntry and bulkCreateEntries' per-item loop — the only two db.insert(entries) sites), so no MCP tool, delivery write, or bulk path can spoof state; transitions are additionally actor-gated using the AuditActor every mutator already receives; workflow guards ADD parameterized WHERE conditions and never bypass buildEntrySchema or verifyRefs; job handlers never write entry data — they only deliver webhooks/emails through the existing logged paths; the two new raw-templated statements (claimDueJobs, the self-join CAS UPDATE) use fixed identifiers and parameterized values only, the same discipline as updateEntryIf and syncUniqueIndexes.
crossGapDependencies: This gap PROVIDES the shared jobs primitive: search-embeddings and payments should depend on G1 (enqueueJob + a new kind in lib/job-handlers.ts + the same drain), not build their own queue — and should copy the run-time re-resolution rule (queued payloads are references, current config is authority).
A change-feed/delta gap could consume the 'entry.transitioned' delivery payload introduced in G4 and the event_action job payloads from G2 rather than inventing a new event log.

The Render migration workstream (infra-ops) owns replacing netlify/functions/jobs-drain.mjs with a render.yaml cron hitting the same POST /api/jobs/drain — the endpoint (including its E_UNCONFIGURED fail-closed contract) is the interface; verify CRON_SECRET is set in the new environment as part of that move's checklist.
The durable rate-limit store (infra-ops pending item) is independent — do not fold it into the jobs table; different access pattern (hot per-request counter vs due-time queue).

A future roles/claims gap (claims-based role presets on the business-logic ladder) is the natural home for refining workflow actors beyond mcp|admin|delivery — e.g. operator-only transitions or claim-matched actors; the actors array is designed to extend, not be replaced.
risks: FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED inside a single UPDATE...IN(SELECT...) over the neon-http driver is believed safe (one statement = one implicit transaction) but MUST be proven in the G1 smoke test before anything builds on it; fallback is a per-row optimistic claim loop (UPDATE ... WHERE id=? AND status='pending' RETURNING).

The self-join UPDATE ... FROM entries old ... RETURNING old.data pre-image trick (G4/G4b) relies on Postgres reading the joined row from the pre-update snapshot — standard semantics, but it gets its own dedicated smoke proof (G4b) with a specified two-step optimistic fallback, because transition-action correctness depends on it.

Delivery is at-least-once by construction (lease reclaim can re-run a job whose handler succeeded but whose finishJob write was lost) — receivers must dedupe; run-time re-resolution bounds the blast radius of config changes to at most one in-flight send, and tool descriptions must keep saying both.

Run-time action matching is by content hash: EDITING an action orphans (skips) jobs queued under the old definition — correct for the kill-switch guarantee but potentially surprising ('I tweaked the subject and the queued reminders vanished'); mitigated by stating it in the events description and surfacing skipped-as-'action removed/disabled' notes in list_jobs.

Workflow actors granularity is actor-type only in v1: 'admin' includes client-role members (saveEntry does not distinguish roles in AuditActor), so a client-role admin user can drive admin-permitted transitions — stated in the define_collection description; per-role/claims gating belongs to the roles gap.

The opportunistic defer() drain runs inside a request-scoped after() task: a slow webhook (3x10s timeouts) could hold the invocation ~35s; mitigated by budgetMs and maxJobs caps, and cron remains the guaranteed drain path. The 120s lease must always exceed worst-case handler time — enforce with a constant comment tying the two.

Timezone math for recurrence uses Intl instead of a cron library — DST edge cases (02:30 on a spring-forward day) need explicit tests; constraining dayOfMonth to 1-28 and four frequency presets deliberately shrinks the surface.

Delayed 'updated' actions lose the previous/changedFields context at send time (only the enqueue-time copy rides along, and only for display) — same gap the CAS path already has; documented in the events description rather than papered over.
Netlify scheduled functions have 1-minute granularity and free-tier throttling — schedule `at` times can fire up to a minute late; stated in define_schedule's description.

Smoke suite seeds via direct SQL (helpers.mjs) — jobs/schedules tests rewind run_at/next_run_at by raw UPDATE and the drain-auth test needs a server instance with CRON_SECRET unset; if the jobs schema evolves, helpers must evolve with it (existing documented smoke gotcha).

openMinor.issue: The jobs dedupe unique index (kind, dedupeKey) is not scoped by projectId — a latent cross-tenant suppression trap. G1-G3 keys embed uuids (entryId, scheduleId) so no practical collision today, but the design explicitly invites future gaps (search-embeddings, payments) to reuse enqueueJob; any future kind whose dedupeKey embeds tenant-chosen strings (e.g. collection names, which are only unique per project) would let one tenant's pending job silently suppress another tenant's job via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Include project_id in the partial unique index — (project_id, kind, dedupe_key) WHERE dedupe_key IS NOT NULL AND status='pending' — or have enqueueJob unconditionally prefix dedupeKey with projectId. Zero cost now, removes the trap permanently.

openMinor.issue: G4 updateEntryIf transition semantics have two spec inconsistencies: (1) an idempotent no-op set (patch sets the workflow field to B when the row is already at B) fails with {ok:false, reason:'conflict'} unless B happens to be in allowedFroms(B), breaking CAS retry idempotency for the most common retry shape; (2) the promised 'entry.transitioned' payload transition:{from:A} is unknowable on the CAS path — there is no pre-read and UPDATE...RETURNING yields the post-image, so `from` cannot be populated as specified.
openMinor.suggestedFix: For (1): include B itself in the compiled IN-list guard (treat B→B as a no-op that succeeds without firing transition actions), or document that CAS transition retries must use the `if` condition on the field. For (2): either declare transition.from as nullable on the CAS path in the payload spec and tool description, or capture the old value in the single statement (UPDATE entries SET ... FROM (SELECT id, data->>'<field>' AS old FROM entries WHERE id=$1 FOR UPDATE) prev WHERE entries.id=prev.id RETURNING prev.old — verify this works over neon-http in the G4 smoke).

openMinor.issue: define_collection following the existing `?? null` persistence pattern (lib/collections.ts:346, 'events: input.events ?? null') means any redefine call that omits `workflow` silently deletes the state machine — dropping create-initial enforcement and transition guards with no plan, no confirm, and no signal in the diff. This is consistent with how events/access already behave, but workflow removal changes WRITE-enforcement semantics (spoofable status again), which is closer to a destructive change than losing an event hook.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Include 'workflow removed' (and 'workflow field/initial changed') in the DefineResult diff so agents see it in the response, and state in the define_collection description that omitting workflow on redefine removes it. Optionally gate workflow removal behind the existing confirm flow since it re-opens status spoofing on collections that relied on it.

openMinor.issue: G1 is a fat M drifting toward L: queue table + lib/jobs.ts (5 functions) + handler registry + drain route + middleware edit + opportunistic defer() nudges in TWO hot routes + a new MCP tool + Netlify function + toml + smoke — 11 files. The nudges are the riskiest piece (a slow webhook job can hold the mutation request's after() task ~35s) and are not needed for independent shippability, since the cron drain is the guaranteed path.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Trim G1 to table + lib/jobs.ts + handlers + drain route + middleware + Netlify glue + smoke (list_jobs can stay — it is the MCP round-trip verification). Move the opportunistic defer() nudges in app/api/mcp/route.ts and app/api/v1/[collection]/route.ts into G2 (where latency first matters for reminders) or a G1b, with the budgetMs/maxJobs caps proven in G1's smoke first.

openMinor.issue: G3's filesTouched omits files it must edit: dispatchEmail is module-private in C:\dev\AgentX\lib\events.ts:102 (the schedule_fire email branch needs it exported), and making webhook_deliveries.collectionId nullable (db/schema.ts:187 is currently .notNull()) ripples through deliverWebhook's params in lib/webhook.ts, dispatchEmail's signature, and refireDelivery (lib/events.ts:174,183 pass row.collectionId typed as string). Neither lib/webhook.ts nor lib/events.ts appears in G3's file list, so the increment is under-scoped as written.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Add lib/webhook.ts and lib/events.ts to G3's filesTouched; specify the export of dispatchEmail (or a runEventAction reuse from G2, which already refactors that dispatch) and the collectionId: string | null type ripple through deliverWebhook/dispatchEmail/refireDelivery.

openMinor.issue: G3 hand-rolls timezone recurrence with Intl ('computeNextRun ... using Intl.DateTimeFormat timezone math'). Computing 'next Monday 09:00 in Europe/Berlin' as a UTC instant requires an offset-search loop and has genuine DST edge cases (non-existent 02:30 on spring-forward, ambiguous times on fall-back) — the design acknowledges the risk but still budgets G3 as M with three new tools, a table, a tick, and this math included.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Ship G3 with timezone: 'UTC' only (reject other values with a fix-hint naming the limitation), keeping recurrenceSchema forward-compatible; add IANA timezones as a later S increment with an explicit DST test matrix. This cuts the trickiest 30% of G3 for a use-case loss most schedule consumers won't notice.

openMinor.issue: G2's dedupeKey (`${entryId}:${event}:${actionHash}` unique while pending) silently pins reminder timing to the FIRST triggering update: subsequent updates neither reset the timer nor enqueue, and the payload's enqueue-time changedFields describe the first update, not the state that ultimately matters. Whether 'after: 3d' means '3d after first trigger' or '3d after last activity' is a semantic choice agents will guess wrong at — and nothing in the specced define_collection events description states it.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Document the fires-from-first-trigger semantic explicitly in the events description ('after starts from the first matching event; later updates do not reset the timer'), and note changedFields in delayed payloads reflect enqueue time. If timer-reset semantics are ever wanted, that is a new knob, not a silent behavior change.
